title: Australian women and children leave Syrian camp hoping to return home
text:
A group of so-called ISIS brides and their children left a camp in northern Syria today, hoping to return to Australia following a failed repatriation attempt earlier this year.

Four women and nine children have been issued passports by the federal government before they left the dire conditions at Syria's Al-Roj camp for what they hope is the final time.

The camp's co-ordinator ﻿Lana Hussein said there is a security procedure that takes up to 72 hours before they are deported.

A group of so-called ISIS brides and their children left a camp in Northern Syria today, hoping to return to Australia following a failed repatriation attempt earlier this year. (9News)

Less than three months ago the entire group of 34 Australians citizens at the camp tried to come home but their escape was foiled by Syrian government forces.

It appears that this time they have overcome that hurdle.

"The co-ordination was perfect," Al-Roj camp director Hakmiyeh Ibrahim told the ABC through a translator.

"It was done between us and the Syrian Government to be able to fly back these families to their country."

The Australian government is required to issue passports to its citizens.

This group is no exception but the government says assistance to bring them back stopped there.

READ MORE: ﻿ Landmark bar attacked by arsonists

A group of so-called ISIS brides and their children left a camp in Northern Syria today, hoping to return to Australia following a failed repatriation attempt earlier this year. (9News)

"The Australian government is not repatriating those people from Syria," Deputy Prime Minister Richard Marles said.

Only one of the women who has been living at the Syrian camp has been temporarily banned from entering Australia.

No other exclusion orders have been issued.

"Tony Burke himself has said 'We do not want them here'," Shadow Home Affairs Minister Jonno Duniam said.

"You are in government, you can do something about it."

A group of so-called ISIS brides and their children left a camp in Northern Syria today, hoping to return to Australia following a failed repatriation attempt earlier this year. (9News)

When terror group Islamic State retreated in 2019, families linked to its fighters were taken to decrepit camps.

The move was something their children had no choice in.

This group's departure leaves another 11 Australian women and children in Al-Roj.

The camp says there are no plans yet for their return.

title: Adelaide family forced to crowdfund boy's life-saving cancer therapy
text:
A family from Adelaide have been forced to turn to strangers and crowdfund for life-saving cancer treatment abroad for their child.

The solution was meant to be found in Adelaide, with a basement on North Terrace designed for a ground-breaking Proton Therapy Unit, which now is only filled with a decade-old empty promise.

In 2024 Levi was diagnosed with stage-four medulloblastoma, an aggressive and unrelenting brain tumour, which came as a shock to both the then-nine-year-old and his mum Jade Elston.

READ MORE: ﻿ Netanyahu reveals he quietly underwent cancer treatment

An Adelaide family have been forced to crowdfund to send their child abroad for life-saving cancer therapy after an unfulfilled promise to bring the treatment to South Australia. (9News)

"I cry a lot," Elston told 9News.

"It just feels like a dream. You don't know what to do, you just feel helpless as a mum."

After years of some of the strongest chemotherapy and radiation treatments, Levi was in remission - twice.

But now, the 11-year-old's cancer has come back.

"They did say when children relapse with medulloblastoma, especially with this third relapse, they often pass," Elston said.

"He's gone through a lot. He can hardly even walk to the toilet, he gets puffed out walking to the toilet."

An Adelaide family has been forced to crowdfund to send their child abroad for life-saving cancer therapy after an unfulfilled promise to bring the treatment to South Australia. (9News)

The only hope left for the family is a treatment called proton therapy, which is specifically targeted to treat childhood cancers.

"We specifically need that because there's no way to operate," Elston said.

That treatment was promised for South Australia back in 2016, with $500 million spent on the Bragg Centre on North Terrace and its purpose-built bunker.

It was meant to house a machine which never got built.

"Let's not assume that, had there not been a delay, this program would have been in place by now," government spokesperson Tom Koutsantonis told 9News.

SA's Health Research Institute took the machine's developer to court and last month won back $35 million over the failed contract.

An Adelaide family has been forced to crowdfund to send their child abroad for life-saving cancer therapy after an unfulfilled promise to bring the treatment to South Australia. (9News)

But that's cold comfort to the Elston family, who are now forced to crowdfund for an overseas trip to Singapore for the life-saving treatment.

Federal Health Minister Mark Butler wasn't available to answer questions, instead telling 9News he's now canvassing other states to potentially host a proton therapy machine.

Whatever happens next, Elston has this message for politicians.

"Pull your head in and get it fixed, children are losing lives," she said.

title: Rebel accused of leaking actor's nude photo amid feud
text:
Hollywood star Rebel Wilson has been accused of orchestrating a cyber attack on the social media account of a rising star which led to her nude photo being leaked.

The Pitch Perfect star is being sued by Charlotte MacInnes, the Australian lead actor of her recently released directorial debut, musical comedy The Deb .

MacInnes claims she was defamed by Wilson in four social media posts which claimed the young actor made a sexual harassment complaint and then retracted it to further her career.

Rebel Wilson is being sued for defamation by Charlotte MacInnes, the star of Wilson's directorial debut The Deb. (Sydney Morning Herald)

In a shocking affidavit filed in the Federal Court, the young star blames Wilson for arranging for her social media account to be hacked and a nude photo leaked.

MacInnes claims her Snapchat account was breached and there was an attempt to hack her Facebook account days after she filed legal proceedings against Wilson in September 2025.

"I believe that this was orchestrated by Rebel," she wrote in her statement.

"It was completely terrifying and caused me a new kind of anxiety."

There has been no evidence tendered to support her claim and Wilson has not yet been questioned about the hack.

It came on the heels of Wilson spreading "malicious lies" in social media posts which left MacInnes crying every day for weeks, the young actor said in an affidavit.

Amanda Ghost co-produced recently released musical comedy film The Deb, which is set in rural NSW and stars emerging Australian actor Charlotte MacInnes. (Sam Mooy)

"I felt as though she was ripping me apart and showing it off to the world," MacInnes revealed.

She had looked up to Wilson but was left feeling powerless after the Hollywood star shared posts with her 11 million followers accusing MacInnes of dishonestly retracting a sexual harassment complaint.

The Bridesmaid actor bullied and intimidated her while falsely painting herself as a whistleblower who spoke up to protect her, MacInnes said.

"I was angry that Rebel claims to be someone who stands up for women … but then was so maliciously and unfairly persisting with a narrative that painted me as a liar, prostitute, sell out, and whore," she wrote in a sworn statement.

It had been heartbreaking to see so many people believing Wilson and to be the subject of abuse and hatred online as a result, MacInnes wrote.

She revealed she made the devastating decision not to attend the premiere of The Deb - her first feature film - because she didn't feel safe around Wilson.

"I continue to feel unsafe in the industry I had once dreamed of, as Rebel is so well known and I do not yet feel I have enough of a voice to fight back," the actor attested.

Her lawsuit centres on the aftermath of a medical episode suffered by the film's co-producer Amanda Ghost at Bondi Beach in September 2023.

Rebel Wilson arrives at the Federal Court in Sydney on Monday morning. The star of Rebel Wilson's film The Deb, Charlotte MacInnes, is suing her for defamation. Monday 20th April 2026 (Sitthixay Ditthavong)

MacInnes helped her back to an apartment they were sharing with others and the pair ended up sharing a bath in their swimwear in a bid to warm up, she says.

Wilson claims her film's star had confided that she had felt uncomfortable after Ms Ghost requested they shower and bathe together, but MacInnes has denied making a complaint.

Ms Ghost told the Federal Court on Friday she was shocked when the A-lister said MacInnes reported feeling uncomfortable and asked her to clarify whether a complaint was being made.

"Charlotte says all good," Wilson informed her after speaking to MacInnes.

"She just meant 'it was a bizarre situation' not that she personally felt uncomfortable."

When Ms Ghost expressed her relief, the first-time director explained MacInnes instantly said there were "zero issues".

READ MORE: Dingoes attack young girl and mother at Western Australia campsite

Charlotte MacInnes arrives at Federal Court in Sydney with her boyfriend Carlo Boumouglbay during a defamation case against actress Rebel Wilson. Thursday, April 23, 2026 (Audrey Richardson/The Sydney Morning Herald)

But the producer agreed she had doubts about MacInnes' honesty after discussing the complaint further with Wilson.

"I was annoyed after Rebel presented a case about her to me," she said.

Ms Ghost was so concerned she decided to avoid MacInnes, instigate a third-party investigation into the complaint and evict the young actor from the apartment.

She came to believe the young actor was telling the truth after meeting with her in person, she told the court.

Wilson will take the stand when court resumes on Tuesday.

title: Advocate threatened with fine for using Adelaide Airport disability zone without permit
text:
Disability advocate and wheelchair user Shane Hryhorec is calling for Adelaide Airport to use a common sense approach after being threatened with a fine for using an accessible pick-up zone without a permit.

Mr Hryhorec posted a video on social media on Saturday of an interaction he had with an airport employee who told him he couldn't be picked up from the designated area.

In the video, which has had more than one million views, he said an employee told his mother not to pick him up in the airport's disability parking permit zone without a permit and threatened her with a $400 fine.

“At that point he was explaining to me that my mum was not allowed to pick me up in the wheelchair disability area even though I’m in a wheelchair with a service animal," Mr Hryhorec said.

Disability advocate Shane Hryhorec has called for Adelaide Airport to change its rules after being threatened with a fine for being picked up from a designated disability parking area. (ABC News: Marco Catalano)

The designated area at the airport has signs which state it's a parking area for disability permit holders.

Mr Hryhorec said he understood that but more common sense was needed when being picked up by family or friends.

“The reason why you have a disability parking permit is to prove you have a disability, but what more proof can you get? I’m in a wheelchair," he said.

" Common sense prevails, doesn't it? "

He said the government only provided him with one permit, which was in his own car, and he couldn't easily supply it to another driver before travelling.

“It’s quite tricky because I just don’t know who’s going to be available to pick me up," he said.

“It’s impossible to have permits for every person that might come and collect me from the airport."

Advocate calls on airport to 'change its rules'

My Hryhorec, who travels frequently for his travel blog, said most of the time he has had positive experiences at Adelaide Airport.

A parking sign at the Adelaide Airport's designated disability parking area. (ABC News)

He said Saturday's experience was a set back for all people with disabilities.

“I felt for my mum having to be in the car with me and having to experience what it’s like, firsthand, to see your son being discriminated against," he said.

“So many other people have experienced this. This is not an isolated incident.

" This is not an isolated incident here in South Australia. It's happening all around Australia. All around the world. "

Mr Hryhorec said independence and dignity were important for people with disabilities that already faced enough challenges when travelling.

Shane Hryhorec frequently travels for his travel blog. (Supplied: Shane Hryhorec)

He called on Adelaide Airport to "change its rules" and have "more empathy".

“Life with a disability is not easy, travelling with a disability is really, really hard," he said.

“We have enough barriers as there is without having to deal with situations like this just by getting dropped off and picked up from the airport."

He said the incident also showed how discrimination could impact other people in your life, and said his mum was quite rattled from the interaction and threatened with a $400 fine.

“I'm hoping the airport doesn’t send us a $400 fine but if they do I certainly will be appealing it."

Airport apologises for 'frustration and distress'

In a statement provided to the ABC, an Adelaide Airport spokesperson said it was aware of a recent experience of a passenger in the dedicated disability pick-up and drop-off zone.

A person with measles left Adelaide for Sydney on December 29. (ABC News: Brant Cumming)

"We apologise for the frustration and distress this experienced has caused," a spokesperson said.

"We are investigating this matter including speaking to the passenger and our employee.

"Pending the outcome of the investigation, we will review our policies and procedures and make any necessary changes.

"We are committed to ensuring a seamless and positive experience for all our customers."

title: Economist turns guerilla groundskeeper to save heritage building
text:
Craig James was dismayed on his daily walk seeing a sandstone 19th-century building falling into disrepair, with boarded-up windows and surrounded by knee-high grass.

Mr James was forced into retirement from his job as an economist two years ago by Parkinson's disease.

Nearby his home in Huntleys Cove in Sydney's north-west is The Priory, a heritage-listed former Marist Fathers home built in the 1840s that has been left unused for 20 years.

Craig James says the grass was knee-high before he started cutting the lawns around the building. (ABC News: Declan Bowring)

To restore the building in Huntleys Cove in Sydney's north-west to its former glory, Mr James became a guerilla groundskeeper for The Priory.

"If you don't use something for 20 years, the potential is for the things to fall off or drop off," Mr James said.

" I didn't want to see the building and its grounds go that way. "

Mr James mowed the lawns, trimmed the edges and fixed up the peeling paint on the window frames with some sandpaper, paint and a brush.

Craig James made television appearances for finance stories as the bank's chief economist. (ABC)

The boards have also been taken down after Mr James brought it up with the council, which has since replaced the broken windows.

"I don't think it's looked better," Mr James said.

He says Hunters Hill Council didn't mind him tidying up the outside of the building but didn't want him climbing ladders to do any painting.

Craig James bought paint and did up the window frames. (ABC News: Declan Bowring)

"I said, 'Look, I'm happy to write a form, affidavit, or whatever you like to call it, that if I ever fall down … I'll never take action," Mr James said.

The council's general manager Nick Tobin said he was supportive of his work maintaining the state-heritage listed building.

"I am in contact with Mr James and met him onsite several times and correspond regularly," Mr Tobin said.

"Any maintenance or works must be undertaken in accordance with the approved Conservation Management Plan and relevant heritage and safety requirements."

The building was named The Priory in 1874. (ABC News: Declan Bowring)

Heritage group fears council relinquishing site

Built in the late 1840s, The Priory started as a farmhouse before being sold to the Marist Fathers to be a base for missionaries.

It was sold to farmer Thomas Salter in 1874 who renamed it The Priory before he sold it to the Gladesville Mental Hospital in 1887.

The NSW Department of Health vacated the site in 2003 but still owns the land and leases it to Hunters Hill Council, which maintains the site.

The building is noted on the State Heritage Register as an example of early settlement in Hunters Hill. (ABC News: Declan Bowring)

The building is a noted example of Victoria Palladian architectural style and of early settlement in Hunters Hill, according to the NSW State Heritage Register.

Other than a failed attempt to turn the building into the cafe, the building has remained dormant for more than 20 years.

Annette Gallard, president of Hunters Hill Trust, an organisation that advocates for local heritage, says she's concerned council was planning to hand the building back to the state government.

"Council, I think, has made the decision that they can't use the building for a business," Ms Gallard said.

"Our theory is that the state government will sell the building off and it'll be in private hands."

At least $6 million is the estimated cost of making the building suitable for occupation. (ABC News: Declan Bowring)

No plans to give building back to the state

However, Mr Tobin from the council said there was "no prospect of the state government paying to take back responsibility of the site"

He said it would cost at least $6 million to bring the building up to a standard to allow for occupation, to pay for the facade restoration, electrical upgrades and fire services.

"Council will shortly be writing to the NSW government seeking assistance with these costs and is looking at other sources of funding to assist in meeting these costs prior to seeking another user for the site," Mr Tobin said.

Hunters Hill Trust would like to see the building reopened for community use. (ABC News: Declan Bowring)

"Any future activation or use of The Priory would be subject to heritage/planning approvals, funding availability, and compliance with the Conservation Management Plan."

" No final decisions have been made at this stage. "

Ms Gallard says she would like to see the building opened up for community use.

"We'd like to see things like a small cafe on site, perhaps a community art gallery with artist in residence.

"It's in such a gorgeous location … it would be a real drawcard for the public."

title: Stilt stunt for the ages as one man attempts half-marathon world record
text:
Santiago Vergara quite literally views the world from on high.

A performer and artist, the Colombian man took to the Ballarat half-marathon today to attempt quite a feat.

Mr Vergara ran the 21-kilometre race on 90-centimetre stilts.

While hundreds of runners took to the streets of Ballarat, abuzz with excitement over their own athletic feats, many stopped in their stretching and limbering up just minutes before the run to take a selfie with Mr Vergara.

Running the event in just 2 hours and 34 minutes, the 33-year-old said the stilt stunt might have earned him a Guinness World Record.

Santiago Vergara's attempt to nab a Guinness World Record was met with cheers and high fives from other runners today. (ABC Ballarat: Laura Mayers)

" The Guinness World Records told me I needed to do it in less than 3 hours and 5 minutes, now I'm going to have to send them all the evidence … but in the official time, I did it. "

Beaming and barely breaking a sweat after the 2.5-hour run on stilts, Mr Vergara said he was thrilled with the result.

"I'm exhausted, I'm tired, I'm really, really sore … but very, very happy," Mr Vergara said.

"I tried to follow the pace of [others] but at kilometre 15 they started to [put in] distance with me and [others].

" The last part of the race was really difficult, and I was almost falling but I could do it — I tried to breathe, and I did 2 [hours] 34 minutes. "

Santiago Vergara ran the Ballarat half-marathon in 2 hours and 34 minutes. (ABC Ballarat: Laura Mayers)

There were plenty of runners and onlookers alike who were stumped at the tall tilt for the Guinness World Record, with many stopping to ask Mr Vergara why he would undertake such a feat.

"I've been walking on stilts for 18 years now, and I love my stilts with all of my soul," he said.

"I'm looking for challenges all the time with my stilts, and not only that but I bring them everywhere.

"The first time I saw the sea, I brought my stilts … I've gone to Machu Picchu with my stilts … I've walked, and climbed mountains, I've done many beautiful things on them."

Mr Vergara was not the first person to run a marathon or half-marathon in stilts.

Michelle Frost ran the fastest marathon on stilts in 6 hours and 37 minutes in 2018 at the London marathon.

Brother and sister duo Charley Phillips and George Phillips held the title before, running the 2012 London Marathon in 6 hours and 50 minutes.

Phillip Leitch, Renae Marimsa, Samson Miller and Alex Gay were a sight to behold during the 5km run on Saturday. (ABC Ballarat: Laura Mayers)

Knights of the round marathon circuit

Mr Vergara wasn't the only adventurous athlete who was gaining attention at the two-day marathon event over the weekend.

In shining medieval metal armour, Phillip Leitch, Renae Marimsa, Samson Miller and Alex Gay were a sight to behold during the 5km run on Saturday.

Despite warm weather and sunny skies, Mr Leitch and his Knights completed the course in under an hour.

"I'm feeling good, everyone pulled up really well. We were a bit surprised, our goal was under an hour — and we managed to keep moving the entire time, no stops," Mr Leitch said.

The four Knights are performers and jousters from Ballarat's medieval theme park, Kryal Castle.

Mr Leitch said they thought they would attempt the feat again next year.

Phillip Leitch is a performer and athlete from Kryal Castle, who says running in armour has helped his fitness levels. (ABC Ballarat: Laura Mayers)

"I imagine it'll be a regular thing for us, there are other knights at the Kryal Castle who saw us do it and might be keen to join, now," he said.

" The amount of support we had from people going down the road, people congratulating us after, it might be a silly thing to do but people really enjoy it. "

Not one to let moss gather on a steel breastplate, Mr Leitch was back to work at Kryal Castle again today, in his armour to battle it out in a jousting tournament.

"There was quite the recovery … but jousting event today, all back to work, situation-normal. The fitness we achieved through training will help us with our jousting, our sport, in the long-run."

Santiago Vergara says he was very tired and sore after the 21-kilometre run on stilts. (ABC Ballarat: Laura Mayers)

Organiser of the Ballarat Marathon Adam McNichol said when Mr Vergara rang to ask if the stilt stunt would be possible, he was not completely surprised.

"I have to say, in the running world, part of the boom in these events is that it's for everyone — from elite athletes, to people with crazy events," Mr McNichol said.

"We absolutely encourage this kind of thing.

"We have to make sure, but it's heaps of fun. We want our event to be super professional, but super fun. We have half a dozen Olympians running, but then we have our man on stilts.

"We are happy for our event to be put on the map in any weird and wonderful way possible."

title: Love story: Bonding with my daughter was amazing, but it took time
text:
Molly, who was in the depths of perinatal depression and anxiety, didn't feel a bond with her baby at first. But a little smile and a wave were the first clues she was falling deeply in love.

Whether it's romantic, platonic, heartbreaking or surprising, our Love Story series is about love in all its forms. Share yours with us.

Before I had my daughter, towards the end of COVID, I suffered some work-related burnout that later led to depression.

I ended up on antidepressants, but a lot of them didn't work — I took six different kinds.

A psychiatrist said I had treatment-resistant depression, because it took so many to find the right one.

Molly says she felt guilty about not feeling a bond with her newborn baby. (Supplied)

The hardest thing was the lack of motivation and just feeling really sad.

I described it to my husband as, "Imagine if every single person if your life died, and how sad you would be".

That's how I felt all the time. There was no joy in anything, ever.

Sign up to the ABC Lifestyle newsletter Get a mid-week boost and receive easy recipes, wellbeing ideas, and home and garden tips in your inbox every Wednesday. You’ll also receive a monthly newsletter of our best recipes.

The psychiatrist told me that I probably wouldn't be able to be pregnant and not be on antidepressants.

But I didn't want to be on the medication forever, so I stopped taking it about seven months before I fell pregnant.

And my pregnancy was great.

If you, or someone you know, needs help: Sands (miscarriage, stillbirth and newborn death support) on 1300 072 637

(miscarriage, stillbirth and newborn death support) on 1300 072 637 Kids Helpline on 1800 55 1800

on 1800 55 1800 Bears of Hope (infant loss support for families) on 1300 114 673

(infant loss support for families) on 1300 114 673 Red Nose Grief and Loss 24/7 Support Line on 1300 308 307

Grief and Loss 24/7 Support Line on 1300 308 307 Lifeline on 13 11 14

on 13 11 14 MensLine Australia on 1300 789 978

on 1300 789 978 Beyond Blue on 1300 22 46 36

I did not feel a bond with her

Birth was not terrible, but not as good as you could hope for.

I was very sick for a couple of hours after birth, so I didn't have that nice bonding time of breastfeeding and cuddling the baby.

On day three, I woke up and just felt that heaviness like I'd had before with depression.

I knew that you can have day-three baby blues, but it felt really bad.

I did not feel a bond with her.

I didn't feel that rush of love they say that you feel when you give birth. I felt more protectiveness over her.

I was sad about the fact that I wasn't feeling that connection, and then I felt guilty about that.

I remember we were driving when she was three weeks old. She screamed the whole way home and I remember feeling sorry for her that I was her mum.

I thought "I'm the worst person for this job. This kid would be better off with someone who loved her more than me."

I didn’t love my baby: The shame of perinatal depression Yumi Stynes speaks to women about their darkest days as new mums; sleep deprived, fearful and feeling alone. She hears how these women came through their suffering and got the help they needed.

I would try and sing to her, and then I would cry, because when I would sing to her, I still wouldn't feel anything.

My husband went back to work when she was four days old. My mum was there every day, but I did all the nights by myself.

I couldn’t sleep, even though I was so tired.

I would get this unsettling feeling when I had time to try and nap while my husband took the baby. Or I’d wake up in a panic because I was on edge that she might need me.

I'm quite talkative and I just wasn't really talking at all. And I didn't really want to eat.

I definitely felt isolated. You spend so much time by yourself, especially during the night-time with your baby.

Also when people would reach out, they'd be like, "Are you loving it?"

And it's really hard to just say, "Yeah, it's great", even though it's not great.

You're not really going to tell people the truth because that's not what they're expecting to hear.

What helped me

Molly says during a family holiday overseas, she began to notice she was enjoying motherhood more. (Supplied)

I had the maternal child health nurse visit and I remember I just started crying and saying that I feel really terrible and I don't know what to do.

She wrote a referral, but I couldn't actually get in to see those people for six months.

At two weeks postpartum, I saw my GP. He was lovely and kind, and wrote me a mental health care plan, but couldn't help me get in to see a psychologist.

When I saw my obstetrician 12 weeks postpartum to get contraception, I spoke to her about the fact that the baby blues just didn't go away.

They found a list of psychologists for me and I got in to see one almost right away. I was four months postpartum at that point.

Treatment was hard. I cried a lot. I remember the psychologist got me to bring my daughter in with me to some of my appointments.

I actually found that quite useful because she would just watch us interact as we normally would.

And then she could reassure me by saying, "Look at the way she responds to you and the way she looks at you, you have a really good bond."

I was really worried about her attachment to me. That helped me get better over time.

Falling in love was amazing

Why perinatal depression is on the rise Perinatal depression and anxiety is one of the leading causes of maternal death in Australia. How can it be turned around?

When she was five months old, we went to the Philippines.

Going overseas, especially to Asia, and taking her on some wild journeys, I kind of realised, well if I can do this, I can do anything.

We did an island-hopping day and my daughter was great that whole time.

She smiled and was waving to everyone, she seemed to really enjoy herself.

I remember looking at her and being really proud and happy that we were here and she was doing so well.

She also started to sleep better and got a bit more settled, so that was another component that helped.

I remember thinking I had more of a bond with her.

Falling in love with my daughter was amazing.

Getting to that stage and realising, "This is what everyone is talking about" took such a long time to get there.

I can't remember what those first months felt like because I love her so much now.

I feel happy. I enjoy spending time with my child more than I do anything else. To the point now where I'm re-evaluating how much I want to work.

And probably the greatest joy that I have is spending time with her.

title: Victoria offers 20pc car rego discount in cost-of-living measure
text:
Victorian motorists will be able to claim back 20 per cent of their car registration fees as part of a cost-of-living measure from the government.

From June 1, eligible vehicle owners will be able to apply for a rebate on the registration fees paid for the 2025/26 year through the Service Victoria website.

Registration for a single car currently costs $930.70 a year, with the discount meaning a driver will receive $186 back.

Victorians will be eligible to claim for up to two cars in their name, but the rebate will only be available until July 31.

The rebate comes after the government announced public transport discounts across the state as war in the Middle East continues to put pressure on fuel prices.

Public transport will remain free until the end of May and then be half-price for the rest of the year.

Jacinta Allan says the cost-of-living measure is necessary as the war in Iran pushes up prices. (ABC News)

Premier Jacinta Allan said it would "provide some relief at a time when working people and their families are looking for this support from their government".

“I do want to acknowledge that this won't solve every problem but it is another step we can take,” Ms Allan said on Sunday.

The car registration rebate is expected to cost the government about $750 million in foregone revenue.

Ms Allan said the blow to the state's coffers would be accounted for in the upcoming budget.

"We can afford it because it's one-off cost-of-living help right now, while we are delivering a surplus," she said.

"Like cheaper public transport, this won't fix everything, but it's immediate action I can take to make a difference."

title: Fremantle Prison gains 'unique' documents about Catalpa captain
text:
One hundred and fifty years after the Catalpa rescue of Irish revolutionaries from Fremantle Prison, historic documents about the famous ship's captain have been donated to the former jail.

In 1876, Captain George Anthony led a covert mission to help six members of the outlawed Irish Republican Brotherhood — better known as Fenians — escape from Fremantle Prison.

Now, Anthony's great-grandson Jim Ryan has donated a trove of documents as part of his visit to WA for the sesquicentennial anniversary of the audacious escape by the revolutionaries.

The collection, which will be shared online by Fremantle Prison next month, includes the bills of sale of the Catalpa itself, Anthony's manuscript, the ship's logbook and a 200-year-old mathematical workbook.

George Anthony's daughter, Ethel, collected local newspaper clippings related to the Catalpa and her father's legacy for decades. (Supplied: Jim Ryan)

It also includes a family scrapbook detailing the life and legacy of Anthony, the principled Quaker from New Bedford, Massachusetts, who risked his life for six Fenians on the other side of the world and was branded a pirate upon his successful return.

"He was considered a pirate by the British, so he could never go to sea again," Mr Ryan said.

"He lost everything."

Rainy day discovery

Mr Ryan discovered his fascinating heritage in New Bedford when he was just six or seven years old.

"I remember it was a rainy day, I kind of snuck up to the attic because I wanted to see what was up there," he said.

"There was all this stuff, old parasols, old dresses, lots of old newspapers and chests and just things on the floor.

Jim Ryan is the great-grandson of George Anthony, the captain of the Catalpa. (ABC News: Andrew Williams)

"So I started picking things up and opening them … and I found these beautiful drawings of a boat.

"My family saved everything."

The treasure trove of artefacts has stayed with him ever since, along with the story of his great-grandfather.

Most of the documents in the collection are at least 150 years old. (Supplied: Jim Ryan)

"I've read every one of his obituaries and every one of them makes this comment: kind, soft-spoken, diligent, caring with people, friendly," Mr Ryan said.

" I learned a lot about being a man from reading about him. "

Yearned for the sea

With a family history stretching back to the Mayflower voyage in 1620, Anthony had seafaring in his blood.

He was nine years old when his father died at sea. He lost two other family members the same way.

However, this did not deter Anthony, who was 15 when he first went to sea and who worked on several boats throughout his life.

Captain George Anthony agreed to help rescue the Fenians because he believed it was the right thing to do. (ABC News: Andrew Williams)

He met John T Richardson, a whaling outfitter who became a surrogate father figure to him and the main force behind his recruitment for the Catalpa rescue.

This mathematical and financial workbook belonged to George Anthony's father, Humphrey, and is around 200 years old. (Supplied: Jim Ryan)

When he met his wife, John Richardson's daughter, Emelina, she demanded he leave the sea behind due to the dangers.

"I think it was hard for him to stay on land," Mr Ryan said.

"I think the love of the ship and the love of the work overcame any fears they may have had."

The plight of the Fenians moved him so much he risked it all.

Several times during the escape, Anthony's calm demeanour and pacifist beliefs played an important part in the mission's success.

Jim Ryan believed this logbook was a decoy Anthony would have handed over to British authorities if the escape failed. (Supplied: Jim Ryan)

A key moment was on the beach in Rockingham, when a local man called James Bell saw the fleeing convicts and raced off to alert the authorities.

One of the undercover Irish operatives wanted to shoot Bell, but Anthony stopped him.

"That was his Quaker background, I think, as well as being smart, saying if we get caught we're going to get hanged [if we kill anyone]," Mr Ryan said.

Collection 'fills a gap' for prison archive

After the Fremantle Prison's closure in 1991, the WA government has turned the former correctional facility into an educational tourism attraction that stands as a monument to convict history in the state.

Conservator Lily Bennion helped with the digitisation process for the collection.

"It is quite unique for us to have international donations, someone from America, that was very rare," she said.

"The collection Jim has provided us is completely unique."

Lily Bennion says the prison has seen a huge increase in public wanting to find out more about the Catalpa rescue. (ABC News: Andrew Williams)

Ms Bennion said prior to this collection the prison only had artefacts and information relating to the conditions at the time the Fenians were incarcerated.

"We don't have any other artefacts from the Catalpa's escape," she said.

"The details of their story from once they left Fremantle, we have very, very little. This fills a huge gap in their story."

Passing the story on

Mr Ryan has travelled to WA several times in the last 20 years for the anniversaries of important events related to Fenians and the Catalpa rescue.

"I've never met more people informed about my great-grandfather than in Fremantle, Rockingham and Bunbury," he said.

In 1920, future president of Ireland Eamon De Valera visited the grave of George Anthony in New Bedford to pay his respects. (Supplied: Jim Ryan)

He said he believed it was his responsibility to further his great-grandfather's legacy and to help pass on the story to the next generation.

"I see myself as mostly a messenger for my great-grandfather," he said.

"It's not about me. It's about me telling the story as accurately and clear as I can."

title: Nelly Korda looking untouchable on record pace at first women's major
text:
Nelly Korda has been forced to settle for a five-shot lead heading into the final round of the Chevron Championship, the largest 54-hole lead of her career.

The American's score of 16-under-par 200 tied the tournament record set by Jennifer Kupcho in 2022, the last year the major was at Mission Hills in the California desert.

Korda had stretched her lead to eight shots until she missed a series of short putts that stopped her from improving on her six-stroke lead at the halfway mark of the first women's golf major of the year.

But the work is not over for the world number two, who looked close to unbeatable for much of her round of 2-under 70 with another clinic controlling her irons on the heavily sloped greens of Memorial Park, even as her competitors did just enough to stay in range.

Australians at the Chevron Championship T20: Cassie Porter — 3-under (70-72-71)

Cassie Porter — 3-under (70-72-71) T29: Hannah Green — 2-under (73-71-70)

Hannah Green — 2-under (73-71-70) T54: Minjee Lee — 1-over (74-72-71)

Minjee Lee — 1-over (74-72-71) T54: Karis Davidson — 1-over (71-72-74)

Karis Davidson — 1-over (71-72-74) CUT: Robyn Choi, Gabi Ruffels, Grace Kim, Stephanie Kyriacou See the full leaderboard.

"I played really solid on the front and then just kind of … not may have lost concentration, but the wind started picking up and then I just put myself into great positions [and] didn't kind of execute really well," Korda said.

The heat and humidity also took a toll, and Korda often walked the fairways with an umbrella to shield her from the Sun.

Patty Tavatanakit, who went 48 holes without a bogey before making one on the 13th, bounced back with a pair of birdies over the last five holes for a 69 to get within five shots.

Cassie Porter leads the Australian charge, but is 13 shots behind the leader. (Getty Images: Sarah Stier)

It was a remarkable scrambling performance for Tavatanakit, who won this major as a rookie in 2021.

Ruoning Yin of China, a Women's PGA champion and former world number one, shot 66 and was six shots behind, along with Pauline Roussin-Bouchard of France, who had a 67.

Cassie Porter is the best of the four Australians, still well back in a tie for 20th on 3-under.

Fifth-ranked Hannah Green shot a 70 for her best round of the tournament so far to move to 2-under in a tie for 29th, while Minjee Lee (71) and Karis Davidson (74) are both 1-over heading into the final day's play.

The ABC of SPORT Sports content to make you think... or allow you not to. A newsletter delivered each Saturday.

title: Shaun Micallef would hate to be immortal, so he wrote a vampire novel
text:
Comedian Shaun Micallef has been thinking a lot about how "transactional" the world can be.

It led him to the idea of vampires, those mythical blood-suckers who quite literally live off others.

The 63-year-old resolved to write his own vampire novel to explore the subject. The result is De'Ath Takes a Holiday, an origin story for the very first vampire, the Comte De'Ath.

"I wanted to explore the nature of immortality and how certainly if I had it, I'd get very bored," Micallef tells ABC Radio National's The Book Show.

In De'Ath Takes a Holiday, Micallef puts the blame for the exploitation in the world squarely on the Comte's shoulders. (Supplied: Ultimo Press)

"That's what's happened to [the Comte De'Ath]. He's kind of done everything now and he goes, Well, what's the point? The whole point of life is its finitude, so he wants to be mortal again if he can be."

Micallef was also inspired by English writer Samuel Butler's 1872 novel Erewhon. Based on Butler's experiences in New Zealand, where he worked in the early 1860s, Erewhon is a utopian sci-fi about the discovery of a new society.

"It's really a satire on Victorian times, mores, social conventions and expectations," Micallef explains.

" I wanted to do something similar about the way the world is now. "

A vampire that pre-dates Dracula

Micallef has always wondered how Dracula — the creature at the centre of Bram Stoker's 1897 gothic horror classic — came to be a vampire.

"So, I've given him an origin story," Micallef says. "This is a proto-Dracula, an Ur-Dracula."

In Micallef's mind, the Comte De'Ath has a voice that's a blend between Alan Rickman and Jeremy Irons. He runs a cotton plantation in Haiti, where he meets hapless insurance agent Roger Bracegirdle, who is visiting to get to the bottom of a 140-year-old claim about a group of drowned enslaved people.

Micallef explains this aspect of the story came from the Zong massacre of 1781, when more than 130 enslaved African people were thrown overboard after the ship ran low on drinking water.

The owners of the ship then made an insurance claim on the murdered enslaved people.

"The insurer said: 'No, they're not property'," Micallef says. "And the court held that these people were property.

" I thought, that's probably the start of it, where there's a legal acknowledgement that human beings can be property and can be used and exploited in that way. "

In De'Ath Takes a Holiday, the enslaved people have turned into zombies and continue to work in the Comte's cotton fields.

"He could have turned them all into vampires because he has that ability, but then of course they don't get to work during the day," Micallef says.

"He gets more bang for his buck if they're zombies."

When Roger arrives at the Comte's home of Alcántara, the vampire asks him to help him become human again. All he needs is a blood transfusion — and a doctor to perform it.

Roger agrees to take him to meet the neurologist Sigmund Freud in Austria, where he will celebrate his honeymoon with his wife, Winsome.

This sets off De'Ath Takes a Holiday's cross-continental adventure where the vampire — and Roger — encounters more than just Freud, but also the likes of Queen Victoria, Oscar Wilde and Henry Ford.

"[He] ends up influencing people like Henry Ford, encouraging him to embrace this industrialised view of capitalism," Micallef says.

"He advises Queen Victoria on the sprawling expanse and nature of the empire she rules over."

It's the kind of story Micallef couldn't tell on TV, where his comedy career started, as a writer on ABC TV sketch series The Big Gig in 1989.

Sketch series The Micallef Program ran for three seasons, from 1998 to 2001. (Supplied: ABC TV)

Since then, he's starred in sketch series Full Frontal and The Micallef Program, helmed comedy news show Shaun Micallef's Mad as Hell for 11 years, and created and starred opposite Kat Stewart in mystery series Mr and Mrs Murder.

"I certainly couldn't do something like [De'Ath Takes a Holiday] on Mad as Hell," Micallef says.

"Like most of the things I try, I start writing and then I think, what is this? Is this a sketch? Is this a TV show? Is this a movie? Oh, this feels like a book."

From comedy to fiction

Writing a vampire novel is perhaps not what most would have expected Micallef to do after Mad as Hell.

Since the show wrapped up in 2022, Micallef has come second on Dancing with the Stars, hosted interview series Shaun Micallef's Eve of Destruction and travelled around the world with fellow comedians in Shaun Micallef's Origin Odyssey.

Since 2024, Micallef has hosted Shaun Micallef's Eve of Destruction, which asks celebrities to share what they would save from their home if it was destroyed. (Supplied: ABC TV)

Next month, he presents a documentary about Australia's obsession with gambling: Shaun Micallef's Going for Broke.

Since Mad as Hell, he also released a memoir, Tripping Over Myself, in 2022, and a collection of comic writing, Slivers, Shards and Skerricks, in 2024.

Micallef describes the process of writing as "solitary" especially after the collaboration, pace and immediate feedback of a weekly TV show.

"I don't know whether I could do this for a living," he says. "That would be a bit lonely, to be honest with you."

But the thought of the book eventually being read by an audience kept him going.

" Otherwise, there's no point to any of it," he says. "All art, even if it's a silly novel, needs an audience. "

That awareness of the audience goes back to his early career as a sketch comedian; he approaches every project through that lens.

"When I wrote this book, I was just being all the characters," Micallef says. "To get into their heads, I had to know how they sounded and how they thought."

But Micallef did need to change his methods slightly. He couldn't get a laugh with a funny face, voice or act-out — but he could make oblique references to everything from ancient Greek playwright Aeschylus to Charlotte Brontë's Jane Eyre.

Mad as Hell won a Logie and four AACTA awards, including best performance in a TV comedy for Micallef. (Supplied: ABC TV)

"I've got to be more careful, more thoughtful and be a better writer," he says.

"My usual way of working is to throw a whole bunch of things up in the air and make sure that I can juggle them.

"If it's a reference that people get, that's fine. If they don't get it, it doesn't matter because it's not the main point of the joke … There's a whole bunch of stuff for people if they want it."

Satirising tradition

Micallef's life in comedy also meant he was more than comfortable leaning into a perceived "silly" side of vampire novels in De'Ath Takes a Holiday.

He suggests Victorian novelists like Stoker and Frankenstein progenitor Mary Shelley were "in on the joke".

"There is an overblown revelling in the ludicrousness of the stories they're telling," he says.

"Dracula is a pretty sexy story too. It's quite rude. I hadn't realised that when I read it as a kid."

Micallef also leaned into that rudeness in his novel. "I like to think there's a lot of comedy in there," he says, much of it coming from the perspective of his narrator, Roger, the insurance agent.

"The narrative voice character is closest to me I suppose, observing what's going on," he says. "But he's not a reliable narrator; he's a bit of an idiot."

Micallef's interest in that style of narration goes back to his childhood love of 1892 comic novel The Diary of a Nobody by George and Weedon Grossmith, about the daily life — and regular humiliations — of a London clerk called Charles Pooter.

"It's a diary written by a very boring person who thinks he's fascinating," Micallef says. "It was hilarious. It still makes me laugh today.

" I think that's always the key to comedy: that the person is not aware of just how inadequate they are. "

He resolved to "parody or quote or just revel" in writing in the style of books like The Diary of a Nobody or the Boy's Own magazines that first inspired his love of reading.

He also drew on the epistolary mode of Victorian novels like Dracula and Robert Louis Stevenson's Strange Case of Dr Jekyll and Mr Hyde, integrating diary entries, court transcripts and even case notes from psychoanalyst Sigmund Freud.

"I try to honour that tradition in this version of a gothic romance novel," Micallef says.

Micallef will next feature on TV screens in Shaun Micallef's Going for Broke. (Supplied: ABC TV)

But it wasn't until the final third of De'Ath Takes a Holiday that he felt truly comfortable writing in Roger's voice.

"I don't want to give anything away, but at the end of the book there's a giant sea creature made of drowned zombies," Micallef says.

"I can't attribute this to anybody else. I'm not parroting anything. I'm not satirising anything.

"I think this is award-winning stuff. I'm going to win the Miles Franklin. Although Australia is mentioned only in passing. I think you've got to have something there about Australia [to win the Miles]. There's like one line. I don't know if that will be enough."

De'Ath Takes a Holiday is published by Ultimo Press.

Shaun Micallef is at Melbourne Writers Festival on May 6-9 and Sydney Writers' Festival on May 22-23.

title: FOI documents show acting PM sought 'due diligence' on NT administrator
text:
Criminal and employment history disclosures, questions about conflicts of interest, and a request for a background check by then-acting prime minister Richard Marles.

Documents released to the ABC under Freedom of Information (FOI) laws have provided a snapshot of the Commonwealth's process in vetting controversial Northern Territory Administrator David Connolly, completed months before a scandal erupted over his social media history.

The former cattle station manager was announced last December as the NT government's pick for the $376,000 per year position — the equivalent of a state governor and the territory's representative for King Charles.

In the weeks after he was named for the role, there was outcry over his past social media posts, some of which were described by federal government MPs as "reprehensible and offensive".

David Connolly's swearing-in as administrator was held in February. (ABC News: Marcus Kennedy)

In January, Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said he was "worried about whether this person is able to properly represent the Northern Territory in a way which is appropriate".

But the FOI documents show that well before then, the federal government had completed what it described as "appropriate due diligence" into Mr Connolly's suitability for the vice-regal role, and that he was assessed to be "appropriately qualified".

The swearing-in was held at NT Parliament House. (ABC News: Marcus Kennedy)

"Mr Conolly's proposed appointment is on the recommendation of the Northern Territory Chief Minister and has been approved by the Australian government," one of the documents reads.

" [He] has over 35 years of leadership in agribusiness and pastoral operations … and is a well-respected figure within the Northern Territory's agricultural sector. "

David Connolly has been backed by members of the NT government, including Deputy Chief Minister Gerard Maley (left). (Supplied: Facebook/Government House)

However, the 32 pages released to the ABC are redacted in parts and the unredacted sections do not contain any specific references to Mr Connolly's social media history.

The Commonwealth's FOI team said its reasons for making the redactions included cabinet confidentiality sensitivities and that the additional information could "cause damage to relations between the Commonwealth and a state [or territory]".

Background checks and shifting timeline

In a letter to Mr Albanese last October, Regional Development Minister Kristy McBain wrote that due diligence on Mr Connolly — as requested by Mr Marles — had been completed.

"Mr Connolly has been assessed by my department as being appropriately qualified for the role of Administrator for the Northern Territory," she wrote.

" I confirm the appointment conforms with applicable legislation and any real or perceived conflicts of interest with the proposed nominee [have] been considered and addressed. "

Correspondence from Kristy McBain was included in the FOI release. (ABC News: Chris Sheedy)

A "private interests declaration" among the documents released to the ABC features 13 questions for Mr Connolly about his criminal and employment history, however it contains no direct questions about social media conduct.

Most of Mr Connolly's answers are redacted, and there is no suggestion he has ever been charged with an offence.

The FOI documents also show the federal government recommended Mr Connolly's swearing-in be pushed back by two months to April 1 this year to allow for the "completion of all required steps and provide sufficient lead time to coordinate the swearing-in ceremony".

Ultimately, that never happened, and Mr Connolly was sworn in at NT Parliament House in late February while a protest against his appointment took place outside.

David Connolly's social media history was picked up during the federal government's vetting process, sources say. (ABC News: Dane Hirst)

Sources have told the ABC Mr Connolly's social media history was picked up and raised with the NT government during the federal due diligence checks, but that ultimately the territory government chose to press ahead with the appointment regardless.

When asked to confirm if this was the case by the ABC, neither the NT nor federal government would comment.

'Respect and impartiality'

Mr Connolly has apologised for the hurt caused by his past online commentary and has been routinely out and about in the community since being sworn in to the role.

Since taking office, he has continued to have a presence on social media via the official NT Administrator Facebook page.

David Connolly inspecting damage from recent wet season flooding in Daly River. (Supplied: Facebook/Government House)

But a spokesperson for Mr Connolly said "the Administrator has no role in administering official communication channels, including social media".

"His priority continues to be serving Territorians with respect and impartiality," they said.

" He has valued the positive and constructive interactions experienced to date. "

The spokesperson said it was "not appropriate for His Honour to comment on statements made by Senators or Members of the Australian Parliament" regarding his appointment.

"Any reflections are a matter for them," they said.

title: 'Waiting for Armageddon': Nimbin preppers ready if world goes up in smoke
text:
There is an air of "I told you so" in Nimbin as residents calmly prepare for what they see as the collapse of the capitalist world.

"Nimbin has been waiting for Armageddon," laughs the town's unofficial mayor, Hemp Embassy President Michael Balderstone.

The latest crisis in the Middle East has not surprised locals in the northern New South Wales town, who have been preparing for the worst since the Aquarius Festival in 1973.

That festival was a turning point for the counter-culture movement in Australia and cemented Nimbin as the country's alternative lifestyle capital.

"I think we've always been ready for it, people in Nimbin — we're poor, live simply and are self-contained and quite self-sufficient," Mr Balderstone said.

Michael Balderstone says "Nimbin has been waiting for Armageddon". (ABC North Coast: Elloise Farrow-Smith)

While other towns might have a community centre, Nimbin has a Sustainable Living Hub.

It serves as a meeting place and education centre for those wanting to live a life less dependent on the global economy.

Coordinator Stephanie Seckold agreed that Nimbin was ready for what may come.

"There isn't a feeling of panic that other people might be experiencing," she said.

"A lot of people in the community have already been thinking about this sort of thing for a long time, whether it's a fuel crisis, whether it's the floods that we had a couple of years ago in the Northern Rivers. A few years before that it was bushfires."

Nimbin's farmer's market is a living example of local food supply and distribution. (ABC North Coast: Elloise Farrow-Smith)

Last month she organised a town meeting to discuss strategies for coping with fuel and food supply disruptions.

"Real prepping is system redesign, not hoarding," Ms Seckold said.

"The thing that Nimbin has that's quite unique is there are an incredible number of independent thinkers."

Among the ideas are a carpooling website and increasing the number of community gardens.

Stephanie Seckold grew and wove the cotton her clothes are made of in this picture. (ABC North Coast: Elloise Farrow-Smith)

Ms Seckold embodies self-sufficiency at all levels and grows her own cotton and silk, which she teaches people to weave into fabric.

"We're not prepping for doomsday — we are working on creating a better way of being," she said.

"It's all about resilience, not survival.

" Instead of seeing a crisis as adversity, I think a more productive way is to think of it as an opportunity. "

Prepping for shortages

There is evidence that Australians are preparing for food shortages.

One of Australia's largest suppliers of canned goods, SPC Global, said there was some indication customers were stockpiling certain non-perishable goods.

Sales of baked beans, canned tomatoes and fruit increased between 12-20 per cent during the fourth and fifth weeks of the Middle East conflict.

In some areas, this concern is sparking a quiet and calm revolution in kitchens and gardens.

Helen Kerr preserves her own food. (ABC North Coast: Elloise Farrow-Smith)

At Collins Creek, just south of the NSW-Queensland border, town planner Helen Kerr is leading that charge.

"My interest in food security started when I was working in Brisbane City Council and did a background paper on peak oil," she said.

" I studied what global oil supplies did to the famines around the world. "

Ms Kerr is pushing Kyogle Council to act at its next meeting on May 11.

"It's an ideal time for the council to pay attention to the priority [food security] because we've got this global oil crisis and we don't know how long it's going to last," she said.

Ms Kerr wants a council strategy to encourage the public to start growing food.

The Collins Creek Food Security Group makes sure nothing goes to waste by drying food such as garlic when it is in abundance. (ABC North Coast: Elloise Farrow-Smith)

"Open up the parks for community gardens — you've got footpaths, front gardens, back gardens, where you can grow food," she said.

"It's something that needs to be done now because of the food security issue and the cost of living pressures that people are facing.

"It takes time to grow food. It's not going to spring up overnight."

Loading...

Seeds in high demand

Ms Kerr walks the talk, teaching others how to preserve their own food, and founded the Collins Creek Food Security Group, which also runs a free seed swapping service for those who want to grow their own food.

That could come in handy — national sales of seedlings and seeds are on the rise.

The Collins Creek Food Security group has a free seed swap service. (ABC North Coast: Elloise Farrow-Smith)

Bunnings said in a statement that demand for seeds was "noticeably higher" than this time last year.

"We've seen a strong increase in customer interest in growing their own food over recent weeks, particularly in edible seedlings and seed packets," it said.

Major distributor Eden Seeds has doubled its staff and has had to turn off the phones while it tries to meet current demands.

Prepping a 'natural reaction'

Tom Doig from the University of Queensland has travelled the world interviewing preppers.

"It's a natural reaction [to the fuel crisis], but I also think what we're moving into isn't anything like an Armageddon," he said.

"Yes, you can buy a whole bunch of cans and rice and pasta now, but you can't buy fresh food three years in advance.

"So unless you actually want to become a farmer and go fully off-grid and have your own food supply, you're going to have to still just pay whatever the money is.

"One of the more exciting and utopian aspects of prepping is when people realise that surviving in a bunker by yourself with some cans of beans and a gun is actually a miserable way of life."

title: Influential Kiwi backs Anzac Bledisloe after Super Round success
text:
Advertisement

SportRugby UnionAustralian rugby Influential Kiwi backs Anzac Bledisloe after Super Round success Iain Payten April 26, 2026 — 1:41pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Save this article for later Add articles to your saved list and come back to them anytime. Got it Share A A A

Leading Kiwi coach Jamie Joseph has backed the concept of a Bledisloe Cup clash on Anzac Day after a successful women’s trans-Tasman Test was staged on Saturday, and further “productive” meetings between Australia and New Zealand were held during Super Round in Christchurch. The potential for more tent-pole events on the trans-Tasman rugby calendar was plain to see over the weekend at a hugely successful Super Round, which was sold out for all three days at Christchurch’s new stadium. Loading After three lacklustre years in Melbourne, the Magic Round-style event paused last year but was revived this season and drew 15,000 visitors to a buzzing Christchurch – including an estimated 4000 from Australia. Super Rugby Pacific is set to keep Super Round in the city for the foreseeable future, but whether it stays on the same long weekend remains to be seen, with renewed momentum behind the creation of an Anzac Day Bledisloe Cup clash.

Advertisement

Citing huge potential for fan engagement and commercial success, Rugby Australia took the proposal to New Zealand in 2024, but after initial support, then-NZR boss Mark Robinson told this masthead last year it was “not viable”. But after regime change at NZRincluding Robinson’s departure, a new All Blacks coach and a new board, new chairman David Kirk said they would re-engage with RA and revisit an April 25 Bledisloe clash. Wallaroos celebrate a Siokapesi Palu try during the O’Reilly Cup match. Getty Images Chief executive Phil Waugh and other RA officials met with NZR counterparts on Friday before Super Round, and informed sources said the discussions continued to be positive and there is hope the NZR board will back it. Waugh declined to comment on Sunday, but speaking to New Zealand radio last week, he said: “We’re very, very optimistic around what some of the outcomes of those conversations may look like.”

Advertisement

The Wallaroos and Black Ferns played an O’Reilly Cup Test match for the first time on Anzac Day on the Sunshine Coast, in front of 6000 people. How a mid-season Bledisloe Cup would work logistically remains to be seen, but an influential source of the initial pushback was said to have come from within New Zealand’s Super Rugby outfits, who are privately owned and didn’t like the concept of losing Test players in the season for a week or two, as NRL clubs do during State of Origin. But Joseph, who coaches the Highlanders and was runner-up to Dave Rennie for the All Blacks coaching job, said he supported the idea. Standing at a fan site in Christchurch located next to the Bridge of Remembrance arch, built in 1923 to honour the region’s fallen soldiers, the senior statesman said he was “really open-minded”.

Advertisement

“A Test match between the Anzacs would be a great occasion, no doubt,” he said. “I played and coached in Australia on Anzac Day, and I know it’s an emotional weekend for the Aussies, and it is in New Zealand as well. As a coach of the Super Rugby team, it’d be disruptive but crikey, lots of things have changed in the last few years, haven’t they? So yeah, definitely open-minded. The concept’s awesome, but there are lots of moving parts.” After several frosty years, much-warmer relations between Australia and New Zealand rugby could also help get the game over the line. Notably, the All Blacks posted a promising post on Saturday with pictures of Wallabies and All Blacks, titled: “Our Trans-Tasman mates.” The Kiwis dispatched all three Aussie sides at Super Round, and cemented themselves in the top four. The Brumbies, Reds and Waratahs are fifth, sixth and seventh and need to finish strongly in the last five rounds to be in with a hope of finishing in the top three and hosting a final. Treyvon Pritchard of the Reds fends off the defence. Getty Images

Advertisement

The fast-improving Force are further back, and they shape as a bogy team for all their domestic rivals. The WA side play the Reds and the Brumbies, and the Tahs twice; starting on Friday night at at Allianz Stadium, with Zac Lomax making his return to Sydney. Related Article Wallabies The Wallabies will have a stacked back line in 2027. This is why Jorgensen is already excited With former league convert Joseph-Aukuso Suaalii also set for a return to the Waratahs side after seven weeks out with a hamstring injury, the clash of the ex-NRL stars will dominate headlines this week. But after an impressive starting debut for the Force last week, Lomax’s credentials as a potential Wallaby will also be scrutinised in the game, with Wallabies Max Jorgensen and Andrew Kellaway in the NSW back three. Former Wallaby winger Drew Mitchell, who played for the Force and NSW, believes Lomax showed his class aerially and skill-wise in the Force’s win over the Crusaders.

Advertisement

“He looked more comfortable than probably a lot of the other [ex-NRL] guys in the early stage, in the first few games that they’ve played,” Mitchell said. “It’s going to be great. If you look at our outside backs, we’re pretty flush with depth at the moment. We’ve got plenty of options. “There’s a lot of guys putting their hand up [for Wallabies spots], and there’s going to be some good players missing out, which is also what you want.” Watch every match of Super Rugby Pacific live and exclusive on Stan Sport.

title: McDonald’s green-lit for ‘world’s coolest street’ after fast food giant’s appeal
text:
Advertisement

NationalVictoriaCity life McDonald’s green-lit for ‘world’s coolest street’ after fast food giant’s appeal Angus Delaney April 26, 2026 — 1:36pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Save this article for later Add articles to your saved list and come back to them anytime. Got it Share A A A

A 24/7 McDonald’s has been green-lit for “the world’s coolest street” after the fast-food giant won an appeal against an inner-north council, despite concerns it will hurt small businesses and tarnish the area’s “cool vibes”. The Victorian Civil and Administrative Tribunal (VCAT) on Friday overturned a decision by Darebin City Council in November to block the development at 323 High Street, Northcote, finding that concerns the McDonald’s would erode the strip’s cultural identity, harm independent businesses and sell unhealthy food were not relevant to the planning application. A render of the proposed McDonald’s restaurant for 323 High Street in Northcote. Darebin Council Agenda VCAT member Michael Deidun said the planning system in Victoria was “somewhat blind to the final operator” and that the planned McDonald’s could not be denied on the grounds that a multinational fast food restaurant might not fit with the street’s current stock of independent vendors. “This tribunal does not have the power to review the corporate approach of McDonald’s, its work practices and ethics, the type of food it produces, its impact on human health, or whether it fits the ‘cool’ vibes of its context,” Deidun said.

Advertisement

The tribunal also found against objections by the council and residents who said increased delivery truck traffic could imperil pedestrians and delivery drivers, that the restaurant should not operate 24/7 and that “rooftop equipment” atop the outlet would be visible from the street. A “convenience restaurant” is generally only permitted to operate between the hours of 6am and 3am, but the tribunal approved McDonald’s bid to open 24/7 because it added just three hours of trading and is in an area where “late night activity is encouraged” and which allows for the “vibrant mixed use of commercial centres”. The tribunal also ruled against residents’ concerns that tinting the glass at the storefront and building a rear car park may decrease surveillance and safety in the area. Following a recent visit to the building, which had been disused since it was gutted by a fire in 2023, Deidun said it was evidently targeted by trespassers and vandals, and that he felt unsafe entering due to the uninviting public walkway. Deidun ruled that turning the site into a round-the-clock restaurant would increase surveillance and safety.

Advertisement

Franc de Petro, who lives off High Street and runs a local Facebook group with 20,000 members, said he was disappointed with the VCAT decision. He said locals and visitors loved how that section of High Street was home to small and family-run businesses, and were worried that the McDonald’s could take patrons away from them. Franc de Petro, a resident of Melbourne’s inner north, outside the location of a planned McDonald’s store in Northcote. Photograph by Chris Hopkins “But for me, it’s more about the feel of the area, it’s the fact that McDonald’s will sort of disrupt the way that street has evolved,” de Petro said. “This will be the first franchise that will go in there.” He added: “I think the locals in the area, the majority won’t want it. I think the people that will want it are probably the ones that don’t live in the area, that are either visiting a live music venue or a bar.”

title: ‘My new favourite player’: Cummins survives onslaught from Indian batting phenom in comeback
text:
Advertisement

SportCricketAustralian cricket ‘My new favourite player’: Cummins survives onslaught from Indian batting phenom in comeback Daniel Brettig April 26, 2026 — 12:43pm Save You have reached your maximum number of saved items. Remove items from your saved list to add more. Save this article for later Add articles to your saved list and come back to them anytime. Got it Share A A A

Pat Cummins went head-to-head with the teenage batting phenom Vaibav Suryavanshi and lived to tell the tale as he led Sunrisers Hyderabad to victory in his comeback from the back injury that severely curtailed his Ashes summer. In an Indian Premier League tournament that has thus far been a graveyard for bowlers, Cummins watched his first ball to 15-year-old Suryavanshi disappear for six over mid-wicket – 11 from his first over suggested a long night ahead. View post on X But Cummins (1-27) locked in thereafter and conceded just seven runs from his final two overs while also yorking the Rajasthan Royals captain Riyan Parag with the kind of delivery that will hearten Australia’s selectors after their skipper’s long layoff. Cummins was certainly impressed by Suryavanshi, who hammered a century from just 36 balls after vaulting to his 50 from a mere 15 deliveries.

Advertisement

“I think he’s my new favourite player,” Cummins said afterwards. “He hits the ball so hard, it’s great to watch, it’s good fun. You’ve got to be right on the money as a bowler because if you’re not, it’s going a long way. He’s impressive, he’s had a great start to his career and I like the way he plays, he takes the game on.” Those close to Cummins believe that the 10 months he spent recovering from back trouble - broken only by a six-wicket haul in the Adelaide Ashes Test - will help to extend his international career ahead of a gruelling calendar in which Australia play as many as 21 Tests in 12 months from August. Pat Cummins in action for Sunrisers Hyderabad. AP “Really happy. Never quite sure how it’ll come out first game,” he said. “I pick it up better than 10 years ago. “The IPL’s fun, you’ve always got to change the fields, use the bowlers, the batters are always making you think. So I enjoyed it, felt pretty natural.”

Advertisement

As for his bowling return in a tournament where scores of comfortably more than 200-plus have become the norm, Cummins quipped that bowlers had to work on their batting. The two Saturday night games saw a combined total of 984 runs scored, underlining how a combination of flat pitches and bold batting were turning this IPL into cricket’s equivalent of the Home Run Derby. Editor's pick Australian cricket ‘You’ve let the fox into the henhouse’: Inside the fight for cricket’s future “I guess you just work on your batting,” Cummins joked. “It’s a good challenge, you can have a bad couple of overs but if you come back and bowl a really good death over that can be match-winning. I actually think in some ways it takes the pressure off us bowlers, knowing that our batting lineup can chase whatever. “The wickets might be flat but it’s only 20 overs, it’s not a Test match where they’re going to bat for five days so I’m OK with that, maximum four overs. It is what it is. We set up our team to try to go big on our batting, and as bowlers it is up to us to try to minimise the runs. The scores look a bit different to a few years ago, but the concept is still the same. “You feel like all four overs are super important, and one wicket, one really good over can actually be as important as a good spell would be in a lower scoring game.”

Advertisement

Chasing 229 to win, Cummins’ Test vice-captain Travis Head departed early, well beaten in a duel with England spearhead Jofra Archer and caught behind, before Abhishek Sharma and Ishan Kishan led the SRH chase to a win that moves them up to third on the IPL table. Punjab Kings, coached by Ricky Ponting, had earlier maintained their primacy at the top of the league by also chasing down a huge score against Delhi Capitals. KL Rahul crushed 152 from 67 balls for Delhi, and inflicted particularly severe punishment on Cricket Australia-contracted swing bowler Xavier Bartlett, who was carted for 69 runs from four overs.
